lenses.guidance: Jacob crosses his hands so the younger Ephraim receives the right-hand blessing (vv 13-14, 17-19) — the pattern that runs Abel-over-Cain, Isaac-over-Ishmael, Jacob-over-Esau, anticipating Christ as divine election against expectation.

Jacob's 'the God who has been my shepherd all my life long, the angel who has redeemed me from all evil' (vv 15-16) gathers the patriarchal experience under the language of covenant redemption. The arc ends with the patriarch's confession.

Jacob blesses through the second-born (v 19) — a Genesis-long pattern climaxing here in the covenant family, foreshadowing Christ the Last Adam (1 Cor 15:45) whose precedence comes by divine election, not chronological birth.
